claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home

Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who's 6 years old, and Blake, who's 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start with some questions about your circumstances:

Do you currently live in the UK with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK at the moment. And neither of the children live with me - they both live elsewhere.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since you don't live in the UK and the children don't live with you, I need to understand your situation better.

Are you contributing financially towards Alex and Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)? And if so, are you contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit would provide (which is £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for each additional child)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards both of them. For Alex, I send about £20 a week - I help out when I can with bits and pieces. For Blake, I send £30 a week to help with their costs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask a few more questions to fully assess your eligibility.

First, regarding your location: Which country are you currently living in?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad at the moment - not in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand you're living abroad. Could you tell me which specific country you're currently living in? This is important because eligibility can depend on whether you're in certain countries (such as EEA countries) or if you're a Crown servant posted abroad.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get too specific, but it's not an EEA country or anything like that. I'm just living abroad - not posted there for work with the government or military or anything.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. That's helpful information.

Now, regarding Blake who is 16 years old - is Blake currently in approved education or training? This is important because Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is still in education - doing A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. 

Now I need to understand more about the living arrangements. You mentioned that both Alex and Blake live elsewhere (not with you). Can you tell me:

1. Who do Alex and Blake currently live with?
2. Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent, and Blake lives with their other parent as well. As for whether anyone else is claiming Child Benefit, I'm not entirely sure - I suppose their other parent might be, but I haven't really discussed it with them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's very helpful.

One final question: Are you a UK citizen or do you have any UK immigration status (such as settled or pre-settled status), or are you working as a Crown servant (for example, in the diplomatic service or armed forces) posted abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not working as a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad as a regular person.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to inform you that **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**. Here's why:

**For Alex (age 6):**
- The main issue is that you do not currently live in the UK. The fundamental requirement for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK, unless you're a Crown servant or living in certain qualifying countries (which you've confirmed doesn't apply to you).
- Additionally, your contribution of £20 per week falls below the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week for an eldest child, which would be needed to establish responsibility when not living with the child.
- Since Alex lives with their other parent who may already be claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim per child, you would not be eligible.

**For Blake (age 16):**
- Although Blake is in approved education (A-levels), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue, the same issue applies: you do not live in the UK and are not a Crown servant or in a qualifying country.
- While your £30 per week contribution does exceed the Child Benefit amount for an additional child (£16.95), the residency requirement is the primary barrier.
- Blake also lives with their other parent who may already be claiming.

**In summary:** The key barrier is that you're living abroad and don't fall into the specific exceptions that allow Child Benefit claims from outside the UK. If you were to return to live in the UK in the future, your eligibility situation might be different.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and lives with their other parent in the UK. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You do not currently live in the UK, and you are not a Crown servant or living in certain qualifying countries. While you can be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep, the contributions must meet the threshold (£25.60 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 for additional children). You are contributing £20 per week for Alex, which is below the £25.60 threshold for an eldest child. Additionally, since Alex lives with their other parent who may already be claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, you would not be eligible to claim for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and is in approved education (doing A-levels at sixth form college), which means Blake would qualify age-wise for Child Benefit to continue until age 20. However, you do not currently live in the UK, and you are not a Crown servant or living in certain qualifying countries that would allow you to claim Child Benefit while abroad. While you contribute £30 per week towards Blake's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £16.95 for an additional child), the fundamental requirement is that you must live in the UK to qualify, unless you fall into specific exceptions such as being a Crown servant or living in certain qualifying countries. Blake also lives with their other parent who may already be claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim for the same child. Therefore, you would not be eligible to claim for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living abroad (not in the UK, EEA, or as a Crown servant) and has two children: Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 16, in approved education). Both children live with their other parent in the UK. The fundamental requirement for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK, with specific exceptions for Crown servants or those living in certain qualifying countries. The claimant does not meet these exceptions. Additionally, the claimant's contributions for Alex (£20/week) fall below the threshold required to establish responsibility as a non-resident parent. Even if contributions were sufficient, only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the other parent likely already claims. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.
